coredata.dc:title: Development and evaluation of a strontium-doped berberine quantum dot-loaded nanofibrous dressing for accelerated wound healing in animal models

coredata.dc:description: This research focused on fabricating a new type of wound dressing made from nanofibers infused with strontium-doped berberine carbon quantum dots (Str-Ber-CQD) to speed up healing and improve the quality of healed tissue. We applied a hydrothermal method using berberine as the carbon source to synthesize Str-Ber-CQD and then blended it into nanofibers made from scleroglucan/polyvinyl alcohol at different concentrations. The results showed that the CQDs have spherical morphology with a size of 3.6 ± 0.9 nm, an average hydrodynamic diameter of 53.2 ± 2.1 nm, and a PDI of 0.350. The XPS and EDX analysis showed that CQDs were composed of C, O, N, and Sr. The fabricated nanofibers have a uniform morphology with promising mechanical strength, swelling capacity, biodegradation, water vapor transmittance rate, and microbial barrier potential. The in vitro biological evaluations confirmed potent antioxidant and antibacterial activities and excellent hemocompatibility/cytocompatibility. The animal studies showed that the optimum structure (nanofibers/ Str-Ber-CQD 5 %) showed impressive results in animal tests involving full-thickness skin wounds. In summary, this study demonstrates that nanofibers/Str-CQD exhibited promising physical, chemical, and biological properties and can be considered a multifunctional wound dressing.

(a) FT-IR spectrum of Sr, Ber, CQD, (b) XRD pattern of CQD, (c) Antioxidant capacity of Sr.Ber-CQD with different concentrations, (d) UV–vis spectrum (scan 200–800 nm) and fluorescence spectrum of Sr.Ber-CQD. All data expressed as mean ± SD (n = 3). Datasets were analyzed using two-way ANOVA followed by Tukey's post hoc test for multiple comparisons. p ≤ 0.05 was considered significant for all analyses. head.ce:title: Development and evaluation of a strontium-doped berberine quantum dot-loaded nanofibrous dressing for accelerated wound healing in animal models

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research focused on fabricating a new type of wound dressing made from nanofibers infused with strontium-doped berberine carbon quantum dots (Str-Ber-CQD) to speed up healing and improve the quality of healed tissue. We applied a hydrothermal method using berberine as the carbon source to synthesize Str-Ber-CQD and then blended it into nanofibers made from scleroglucan/polyvinyl alcohol at different concentrations. The results showed that the CQDs have spherical morphology with a size of 3.6 ± 0.9 nm, an average hydrodynamic diameter of 53.2 ± 2.1 nm, and a PDI of 0.350. The XPS and EDX analysis showed that CQDs were composed of C, O, N, and Sr. The fabricated nanofibers have a uniform morphology with promising mechanical strength, swelling capacity, biodegradation, water vapor transmittance rate, and microbial barrier potential. The in vitro biological evaluations confirmed potent antioxidant and antibacterial activities and excellent hemocompatibility/cytocompatibility. The animal studies showed that the optimum structure (nanofibers/ Str-Ber-CQD 5 %) showed impressive results in animal tests involving full-thickness skin wounds. In summary, this study demonstrates that nanofibers/Str-CQD exhibited promising physical, chemical, and biological properties and can be considered a multifunctional wound dressing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Any damage or disturbance to the normal function and anatomical structure of skin tissue is referred to as a wound, according to international criteria. Acute wounds (such as trauma-related wounds, full-thickness wounds, etc.) and chronic wounds (such as diabetic wounds and pressure ulcers) are the two types of wounds. Hemostasis at the site of damage, inflammation, proliferation, and regeneration are the four distinct but overlapping phases of acute wound healing (such as full-thickness wounds), with healing indicators appearing two to four weeks later [1–3]. The rapid wound-healing process disruption of acute wounds can lead to chronic wound formation. One of the critical factors in the molecular regulation of wound healing pathogenesis is the regulation of inflammation (reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation and oxidative stress) at the wound location. Increased ROS Despite of Preventing infection, can cause cellular damages and delay in wound healing [4].

More sophisticated wound healing techniques are required than conventional ones like cotton gauze dressings, etc., because of the intricate texture of the skin and the significance of quicker wound healing. Recent developments in a number of scientific fields, including biology, tissue engineering, nanotechnology, etc., have greatly aided in the creation of more advanced wound healing techniques, which in turn have produced new generation dressings like nanofibers [1].

In addition to new materials synthesized with nanomaterials science and nanotechnology, such as carbon quantum dots (CQD). The use of nanofibrous dressings, which have unique properties like proper elasticity, high exudate absorption, vapor exchange ability, preventing the passage of external pathogens to the wound site, maintaining moisture and temperature of the wound site, high blood compatibility, biodegradability, etc., has resulted in the emergence of new generation nanofiber dressings that have led to faster and higher quality wound tissue healing when compared to traditional models [5,6].

Nanofibers were created using electrohydrodynamic electrospinning technology, which uses a powerful electric field to turn a polymer solution (such as PVA polymers or scleroglucan) into three-dimensional nanometer fibers that can contain bioactive substances like strontium, berberine quantum dots, and others [5,7]. Two polymers, scleroglucan and polyvinyl alcohol, were employed in this investigation. Some fungi, such as Sclerotium rolfsii, Sclerotium glucanicum, Epicoccum nigrum, and others, produce scleroglucan, a β-glucan exopolysaccharide [8,9]. A class of glucans with a similar structure is referred to as scleroglucan. This nonionic polysaccharide has a high molecular weight and is derived from triple repeats of (1,3)-β-d-glucopyranosyl units, each featuring a side branch of (1,6)-β-d-glucopyranosyl. The presence of these branches in the scleroglucan structure significantly enhances its solubility in water. Owing to its unique rheological properties, stability across a broad pH range, high thermal resistance, resistance to hydrolysis, and hydrophilicity, scleroglucan has been widely utilized in biotechnology, particularly in the production of composite polymers. The polymer's pronounced hydrophilicity is a key advantage, making it highly effective for use in wound dressings, where it enhances exudate absorption and helps maintain skin moisture [8–10].

Following their initial discovery by Ekimov and Onushenko, quantum dots have since found practical clinical and biomedical uses, such as wound care and aiding in the healing and repair of wounds. Numerous studies have demonstrated that transforming organic chemicals from their bulk form into quantum dots might enhance their biological effects. Additionally, because of their enormous surface area, the quantum dots are more stable than the bulk state. For clinical applications, tissue scaffolds, nanofibers, etc., can have their mechanical and physical strength increased by quantum dots [11–13].

Shengli Lu et al. stated that CQDs derived from specific medicinal plants can eliminate Gram-positive bacteria such as methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus. CQDs can exhibit enzyme-like activity similar to catalase and superoxide dismutase for scavenging ROS that helps to control the side effects of infection and oxidative stress during wound healing. These effects were manifested in wound healing, including improved collagen disposition and epithelial regeneration [14].

Because of their special qualities, which include antibacterial and antioxidant qualities, a role in biological processes of angiogenesis and signaling during the wound healing phase, the capacity to reinforce the structure of frameworks and nanofibers, etc. (due to their high specific surface area), CQDs have garnered a lot of interest in the production of nanofibrous wound dressings. According to a number of studies, adding CQDs to novel nanofibrous wound dressings made of natural polymers can improve their mechanical and physical characteristics, increase their capacity to absorb water, and speed up the in vivo wound healing process [6,11–13,15].

Berberine is a natural chemical utilized in herbal therapy, particularly traditional Chinese and Indian medicine, having optimal qualities for use in the wound healing process. Several plant species, especially Berberis vulgaris, contain the yellow isoquinoline alkaloid chemical berberine, which has a variety of therapeutic uses. The impact of berberine on the healing and improvement of diabetic wounds in an animal model was demonstrated in a study by Pashaei et al. compared to the control group. According to research, berberine is a bioactive substance with strong antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant qualities [16–21].

Studies on the effects of herbal medicines on wound healing are becoming more and more important in current therapies due to the growing need for efficient wound treatments and the dearth of appropriate and efficient medications for wound healing. Berberine is a desirable alternative for mixing with nanofibers to create a new generation of wound dressings because of its antibacterial, antioxidant, and inflammation-reducing properties, which improve the skin tissue repair process. In the present study, we used Berberine (Ber) as the carbon source of carbon quantum dots (CQD) and the resultant structure was named as Per-CQD. In order to aid in and speed up wound healing in a rat animal model, the goal of this work is to develop a nanofibrous wound dressing that contains strontium-doped quantum dot berberine (Ber-Sr-CQD).

Berberine hydrochloride, PVA, glutaraldehyde, and DPPH reagent were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were purchased from Gibco (USA). Human dermal fibroblast (HDF) cells were obtained from the Pasteur Institute (Iran). ketamine and xylazine used for animal anesthesia purchased from Alfasan(Iran). Deionized water was used throughout the experiments.

For the synthesis of strontium-doped berberine quantum dots by the hydrothermal method, first, 40 mg of berberine was added to 40 ml of distilled water and placed on a stirrer at 200 rpm and 40 °C for one hour. Then 4 mg of strontium was added to the solution and stirred (200 rpm) for one hour. The solution was transferred to a thermal reactor and placed in a thermal oven at 170 °C for 12 h. The synthesized solution was centrifuged (10 min at 12,000 rpm, and the supernatant was transferred to a new container) and dialyzed (with a special 0.22 μm dialysis bag for 24 h in a one-liter beaker and a magnet rotation of 100 rpm, and the distilled water in the beaker was replaced every six hours) to remove particles larger and smaller than the desired range [13].

TEM microscope images of Sr.Ber-CQD were obtained by Philips EM 208S 100 KV microscope made in the Netherlands. UV–Vis spectra from 200 to 800 nm were obtained by spectrumlab-53 device made in Iran. To prove the excitation and emission properties of the synthesized quantum dots, the fluorescent spectra were examined by a fluorometer (FP-6200, Japan). After centrifugation and dialysis of the synthesized solution, to prove the uniformity and homogeneity of the synthesized particle size, the size and surface charge of Ber-CQD and Sr.Ber-CQD particles were measured by DLS and zeta potential tests using an SZ-100 V2 analyzer (Japan), respectively. The synthesized material's crystal structure and physical properties were obtained by X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns (Bruker, AXS D8 Advance, Germany). The experiment was conducted in the 2θ range of 10°–80° and the step size of 0.08° s−1 with monochromatic CuKα radiations (λ = 1.54056 Å) generated in the current and voltage current of 40 mA and 40 kV, respectively. FT-IR analysis (Spectrum 65, USA) was used to investigate the chemical bonds, molecular interactions, and especially the type of functional groups involved in the interactions between materials [12,22].

Ber-CQD's antioxidant capability was assessed using the DPPH assay (Sigma-Aldrich, USA). Vitamin C served as a reference in this investigation. 1 ml of 0.1 mM DPPH solution was mixed with 3 ml of Sr.Ber-CQD at concentrations of 1000, 3000, 5000, and 10,000 μg/ml. The mixture was then allowed to sit at room temperature in the dark for 30 min. After measuring their absorbance at 517 nm, the antioxidant activity and DPPH inhibition [23] were calculated using the following formula:
DPPH =
A bsControl − Abs Sample
Abs Control
∗ 100
AbsControl: DPPH absorption in the absence of sample (absolute ethanol as control group) AbsSample: DPPH absorption in the presence of different percentages of Sr. Ber.

XPS analysis is one type of analysis that relies on how X-rays interact with the sample. One effective method for assessing the sample surface is XPS. XPS analysis is a quantitative-spectral method that allows for the extraction of the element types and chemical compositions present on the sample surface. About 5 mg of powdered Ber-CQD and Sr.Ber-CQD were prepared for XPS testing (BESTEC, EA10, Germany) and delivered to a specialized laboratory for this investigation [24].

PVA (molecular weight 44.05 g/mol) and SG polymer (>1000 kDa) were utilized in the nanofiber production process. 500 mg of PVA and 25 mg of SG were first added to 5 ml of distilled water to create a 10 % by weight PVA solution. The mixture was then constantly stirred for an hour at 300 rpm and 80 °C until all of the PVA had been dissolved. Next, 0, 5, 15, 25, and 50 mg of Sr.Ber-CQD were added to separate polymer solutions, respectively, to create nanofibers with percentages of 0, 1, 3, 5, and 10 % of Sr.Ber-CQD relative to the PVA polymer. For full dissolution, 200 μl of 1 M NaOH solution was added to the solutions, and they were then stirred for 24 h at 100 rpm and 40 °C. After allowing enough time for the solution to cool, the bubbles were eliminated by subjecting it to ultrasonic waves for ten minutes. The electrospinning procedure was performed utilizing a electrospinning device (Fanavaran Nano-Meghyas, Iran) [25]. A 10 cc syringe was filled with polymer solutions in varying percentages. The syringe was then operated at an injection rate of 0.8 ml/h while being powered by a 20 kV voltage. The nanofibers of the polymer that was discharged from the nozzle tip were gathered at a distance of 15 cm on a collector that rotated at 700 rpm. A layer of aluminum foil was placed over the collector to make it easier to remove the produced nanofibers. To ensure they were entirely dry, the produced fibers were kept at 37° for a whole day. The nanofibers were placed in a special glass jar and subjected to glutaraldehyde vapor (Sigma Aldrich, USA) for a whole day in order to crosslink them (Scheme 1 ). The nanofibers were then taken out of the glass jar, cleaned of glutaraldehyde vapor using distilled water, and allowed to dry entirely at 37° in an incubator.

Scanning electron microscopy tests, porosity, tensile, weight loss, swelling, water vapor transmission rate, microbiological penetration test, blood compatibility assay, and other methods were used to assess the secondary characteristics and morphology of cross-linked nanofibers [26,27].

Utilizing scanning electron microscopy (SEM) imaging, the structural and morphological properties of nanofibers containing varying proportions of Sr.Ber-CQD were examined. The nanofibers were employed for energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDAX) and SEM imaging after being thoroughly dried using a freeze drier (Operon, FDB-5503, Italy). To ascertain the proportion of elements in solid samples, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, or EDAX, is utilized. By analyzing the distinct X-ray energy released from the sample, this study can determine the type of element and its weight or atomic percentage. (VEGA3, TESCAN, Czech Republic) and (Tescan-Vega2, Czech Republic) were used to obtain SEM pictures and EDAX, respectively [5,28].

In addition to analyzing the crystal structures and crystal defects, XRD analysis is utilized to determine the compounds and phases that are present in the materials. The PANalytical (XPert PRo MPD) instrument was used to obtain the corresponding XRD patterns [29].

Examining the mechanical strength of nanofibers—particularly nanofiber dressings—against the different stresses that are produced during application is crucial. Because of their high crystal lattice modulus, PVA nanofibers have a high tensile strength and good resistance to continuous abrasion. The STM-20 universal tensile strength testing machine (SANTAM, Iran) was utilized in this test. Samples of electrospun nanofiber dressings containing varying amounts of Sr.Ber-CQD (0, 1, 3, 5, and 10 %) were sliced to 10 cm in length and 2 cm in width. The nanofibers were adhered to aluminum foil frames, which were chopped before the stretching process began, in order to correctly load the fibers in the load cell. The strain rate of the machine was 10 mm/min with a force of 10 N until the samples were cut [30]:

The resulting electrospun nanofiber's chemical structure and bonding conditions were examined using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). FT-IR experiments were conducted using Spectrum 65 USA. The electrospun nanofiber and its constituent parts were examined for alterations, modifications, and the creation of new bonds. The materials (freeze-dried and completely dry) were first ground separately in a mortar, and 1 mg of the powdered material was evenly distributed in 100 mg of KBr. This was then mechanically pressed into a compact disk with a diameter of 3.5 mm, and it was immediately examined using an FTIR spectrometer equipped with the proper module.

When assessing the effectiveness of nanofibers in absorbing exudate during wound healing, the water absorption capacity or swelling ratio—which is often measured by gravimetry—is a crucial characteristic. Briefly, a sensitive digital scale was used to quantify the dry weight of nanofibers cut into 2 × 2 cm pieces with varying percentages of 0, 1, 3, 5, and 10 % of Sr.Ber-CQD. These pieces were then left at room temperature in PBS buffer, which had a pH of roughly 7.4. Then, at specific time intervals, they were taken out of the buffer and after removing the excess buffer on the surface of the pieces with an absorbent tissue, the weight of the pieces was determined and they were returned to the buffer container. The exudate absorption ability was calculated using the following formula [31,32].
Swelling Ratio
%
=
Wm 1 − Wm 0
Wm 0
× 100
In this equation, Wm1 is the weight of the sample after swelling and Wm0 is the weight of the dry sample before swelling.

When a dressing is biodegradable, it can be readily removed from the wound without contaminating it or causing damage. One criterion for assessing the dressing's biodegradable behavior in gravimetric testing is the weight loss of the dressing with time. Nanofiber pieces of 2 × 2 cm were cut with percentages of 0, 1, 3, 5, and 10 %. Their dry weight was determined after they had completely dried for 24 h at 37° in an incubator. Thereafter, they were submerged in PBS buffer (pH 7.4), taken out at specific intervals (3, 7, 10, and 14 days), let to air dry in the incubator, and their secondary weight was calculated. The following formula [32] was used to determine the nanofibers' weight loss:
Weight loss
%
=
Wm 0 − Wm 1
Wm 0
× 100
In this equation, Wm1 is the weight of the sample after immersion in PBS and re-drying, and Wm0 is the initial weight of the dry sample before immersion.

The porosity of electrospun dressings is one of the features of nanofibers that can influence their physical attributes and use. Using the gravimetric approach, 2 × 2 cm pieces of nanofiber were cut from nanofibers containing 0, 1, 3, 5, and 10 % Sr.Ber-CQD. The pieces were then left in pure ethanol at ambient temperature for a whole day. Then, after removing the pieces from the ethanol and removing excess ethanol from the surface of the pieces with the help of an absorbent tissue, the weight of the pieces (W1) was measured by a digital scale. The pieces were placed in an incubator at 80° for 24 h until the ethanol was removed and the fibers were completely dry, and their net dry weight (W2) was measured. The following formula was used to calculate the porosity [33]:
Porosity
%
=
W 1 − W 2
ρALd
× 100
where is the wet weight with pure ethanol after 24 h (w1), the dry weight of the fiber before immersion in ethanol (w2), the density of pure ethanol g/cm3 (ρ), the area of the nanofiber cm2 (A), and the thickness of the sample cm (Ld).

To have a better knowledge of the properties of nanofiber dressings, it is imperative to measure their water vapor transmission rate. The Water Method, which involves weighing containers filled with distilled water at pre-arranged intervals, was applied in this investigation. A container with distilled water without a lid served as the control group. The mouths of the 1.5 cm-diameter containers, which contained 5 ml of distilled water with synthetic nanofibers at percentages of 0, 1, 3, 5, and 10 % of Sr.Ber-CQD, were fully sealed. For a full day, the containers were maintained at 37°. WVTR was computed using the following formula by weighing the containers both before and after they were exposed to 37°. All steps were performed in triplicate.

Weight change in 24 h in terms of hours (W), container opening (A)
WVTR
g / m 2 / 24 h
=
−
W 2 − W 1
A

RBC membranes breaking down and releasing cellular components like hemoglobin is known as hemolysis. The anticoagulant EDTA was added to tubes containing human blood that had been prepared strictly according to hygienic guidelines. Free hemoglobins and cells lysed during blood collection were eliminated by washing the blood three times with normal saline and removing the supernatant that might have included free hemoglobins. Lastly, normal saline was used to dilute the blood 1:1.

Synthesized nanofibers in percentages of 0, 1, 3, 5, and 10 % were cut into 1 × 1 cm pieces and put in a container with 2 ml of washed blood at 37 °C for an hour. The cleaned blood was diluted with distilled water to create a positive control, which resulted in total blood lysis. Additionally, no hemolysis was seen in the negative control group since the cleaned blood was diluted with regular saline, and no other chemicals were introduced. After an hour, the samples were centrifuged for 15 min at 3000 rpm, and the supernatant was gradually moved to a 96-well plate so that a microplate reader (BioTek Synergy, Synergy-HTX, USA) could measure the absorbance at a wavelength of 542 nm. To calculate the amount of hemolysis, the results were put into the following formula [34]:
Hemolysis assay
%
=
T sample − N control
P control − N control
× 100
The absorption of the sample containing nanofibers with different percentages (Tsample), the absorption of the negative control group (Ncontrol), and the absorption of the positive control group (Pcontrol).
The first step in pathogenesis and wound damage is bacterial invasion and wound contamination. Therefore, the designed nanofiber dressings must be able to resist this microbial invasion.

Tryptone Soy Broth (TSB) culture medium and glass vials were first autoclaved. Both sides of the nanofibers were subjected to UV light for 20 min in order to sanitize them. Each vial was filled with 5 ml of sterile culture medium, and the vials were sealed entirely with sliced nanofibers (with varying proportions of Sr.Ber-CQD). As positive and negative control groups, respectively, one vial with a tight plastic cap and one without a cap were used. Every container was left in the open laboratory setting for seven days at room temperature. The absorbance of the TSB culture medium was assessed at a wavelength of 600 nm on days three and seven. Also, to confirm the results after days 3 and 7, the culture media inside the vials were cultured on blood agar and EMB culture media and the results were reported in the form of images [33].

The antimicrobial activity of the synthesized nanofibers was investigated using the agar diffusion assay. According to the studies conducted and confirmation of the high antibacterial ability of berberine, in order to confirm this ability in quantum dots synthesized with berberine, on Mueller Hinton Agar (MHA) plates previously cultured with E. coli (ATCC 25922), S. aureus (ATCC 10917) bacteria with half McFarland concentration (a reference for adjusting the turbidity of bacterial suspensions in test tubes in order to equalize the number of bacteria in different plates), disks with a diameter of 6 mm and a thickness of 1 mm were prepared from nanofibers with different percentages of Sr. Ber-CQD and were placed under a microbiology hood using sterile forceps at a distance of approximately 30 mm from the edge of the plate and 35 mm from each other on the surface of the plate. E. coli was used as a representative of gram-negative bacteria, and S. aureus was used as a representative of gram-positive bacteria. Finally, the plates were inverted to prevent sweating of the plate surface, and the results were evaluated after 18 h. The diameter of the zone of no growth was measured 3 times, and the inhibition was reported as the average of the zone diameter [30,33].

The antioxidant capacity of the nanofibers synthesized with different percentages of Sr.Ber-CQD was evaluated by the 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) test. Absolute ethanol was used as the control group.

Briefly, 2 × 2 cm pieces of nanofibers were mixed with 1 ml of DPPH and incubated for 30 min at 37 °C in the dark. The DPPH solution without nanofibers was considered as the control group. Then, the absorbance of the samples was measured at a wavelength of 517 nm and used in the following formula to calculate the antioxidant capacity [30]:
DPPH
%
=
Abs
Control − Abs
Sample
Abs
Control
× 100
where the control absorption (Abs control) and the absorption of nanofibers with different percentages of Sr.Ber-CQD (Abs sample) are.

MTT and scratch tests were employed to examine the produced nanofibers' impact on cells. First, DMEM (Gibco, USA) supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (Gibco, USA) was used to cultivate human dermal fibroblasts (HDF) cells (Pasteur Institute, Iran). Penicillin and streptomycin (Pen/Strep) antibiotics were added to the culture medium at a volumetric rate of 1 % in order to prevent bacterial contamination. For two days, the cells were maintained at 37 °C with 5 % CO₂.

One of the most crucial tests for assessing the caliber of the dressings is whether or not the nanofibers are harmful to cells. For this investigation, the indirect-MTT test was employed. After being sterilized by 20 min of exposure to UV light, nanofibers containing varying percentages of 0, 1, 3, 5, and 10 % Sr.Ber-CQD were extracted and placed in DMEM culture media. 100 μl of DMEM culture medium was supplied to each well of 96-well plates after 5000 cells were first taken for each well. The cells were then allowed to develop and attain the proper density for 24 h.Then, the DMEM culture medium was replaced with nanofiber extract and at 24, 48 and 72 h, the culture medium on the cells corresponding to each time point was removed and 10 μl of MTT (0.5 mg/ml) was added to the cells and the cells were kept for 4 h at 37° in the dark. Then the supernatant was removed and 100 μl of DMSO was added to each well and gently shaken to mix well. Three wells without any treatment were considered as the control group. After about 30 min, the absorbance of the wells was measured at a wavelength of 570 nm using an ELISA reader (BioTek, EPOCH, USA) and the results were put into the following formula to determine cell viability [33]:
Cell viability
%
=
OD
treatment
OD
control
× 100

20,000 HDF cells were transferred to 24-well plates and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 to form a monoconfluent cell layer at the bottom of the wells with a density of 90 %. The cells at the bottom of the wells were gently scratched with a yellow sampler tip and washed twice with warm PBS to remove the remains of damaged and detached cells from the medium, and culture medium containing nanofiber extract (with different percentages) was added to the wells again.

To evaluate cell migration for treatments with different percentages of Sr.Ber-CQD, images were taken using a camera microscope (OPTIKA, Italy) at times 0, 12 and 24 and evaluated using Image J software. The information obtained to determine the rate of wound closure was put into the following relationship [33]:
In vitro
wound closure
%
=
Dt 0 − Dti
Dt 0
× 100
where Dt0 is the scratch size at time zero and Dti is the scratch size after predetermined times.

A full-thickness excision wound model was made in Wistar rats (2 months old, weighing approximately 220 g) in order to assess the wound healing capacity of nanofibers in an animal model [35]. The animals were kept on a regulated diet after being acquired from Hamadan University of Medical Sciences' Laboratory Animal Breeding Center. (Hamad University of Medical Sciences Ethics Committee Approval Code IR.UMSHA.AEC.1402.013).

The rats were first given an intraperitoneal injection of a ketamine and xylazine mixture (Alfasan, Iran) at 80 mg/kg and 10 mg/kg, respectively, to induce severe anesthesia in order to produce a wound. After that, an alcohol pad was used to properly sterilize and shave the hair between the shoulder blades. In order to preserve the subcutaneous muscle layer of the panniculus carnosus, a circular wound (1 cm2) was made using surgical scissors in accordance with the full-thickness wound creation procedure. Four groups of animals were formed: 1. The control group (no therapy) 2. Model group wearing a dressing that is sold commercially (COMFEEL brand) 3. Use a nanofiber dressing that contains no Sr. Ber-CQD. 4. Use a nanofiber dressing that has the ideal amount of Sr. Ber-CQD.

Only sterile gauze was used to clothe the control group. Synthetic nanofibers were applied to the wounds of groups three and four to sterilize them after 20 min of exposure to UV radiation on both sides. When a fibrin layer developed on the wound, the dressings were taken off to let the lesion heal naturally. The dressings were replaced on days two and five.

A digital camera was used to capture pictures of the wounds on days 0 (the day of surgery), 3, 7, 10, and 14 in order to evaluate the wound healing process. Image J software was used to analyze the photos. The wound closure results were expressed as a percentage using the following formula [36]:
Wound closure
%
=
T 0
wound area − Tt
wound area
T 0
wound area
× 100
where T0 is the initial wound area and Tt is the wound area at predetermined times.

Two rats from each group were killed on day 7 for histological investigations, and three more rats from each group were murdered on day 14 (by intracardiac injection of a high dosage of ketamine/xylazine). Samples were obtained from the healthy wound margin and the wound region. Histopathological samples were transported to a specialized pathology laboratory for Masson trichrome (MT) and hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) staining after being put in 10 % formalin [36]:

Data analysis was conducted using GraphPad Prism version 9 (GraphPad Software, Inc.). Statistical techniques suitable for the type of data were applied to assess statistical differences between various groups. Tukey's post hoc test and one- and two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) were employed to investigate statistical differences between several groups. All in vitro and in vivo investigations, as well as characterization trials of nanoparticles and nanofibers, were conducted in triplicate, with (*) p < 0.05, (**) p < 0.01, (***) p < 0.001, and (****) p < 0.0001 being the statistical significance levels.

We evaluated the morphology and size of the CQDs using TEM imaging, and the results are presented in Fig. 1 . We found that the CQDs have a partially spherical morphology, homogeneously monodispersed, with a diameter of 3.6 ± 0.9 nm, and with no form of aggregation. The size distribution histogram shows that most CQDs have a size in the range of 3.0 to 4.5 nm. Previous studies showed that most of the synthesized CQDs have a size lower than 10 nm. Praseetha et al. [37] applied a one-pot microwave method to synthesize fluorescent CQDs from almond resin, Prunus dulcis. They reported that the CQDs have an average diameter of around 2.5 nm. Ferjani et al. [38] used waste from Avocado peels to synthesize CQDs using a one-step hydrothermal method. They reported that the CQDs have an average size of around 2.9 nm.

Ber-CQD has an average hydrodynamic diameter of 53.2 ± 2.1 nm and a size Polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.350, according to the size and PDI examination results of the CQD. Sr.Ber-CQD had an average size of 46.5 ± 2.4 nm and a PDI of 0.403 (Fig. 2 A, C). The Ber-CQD and Sr.Ber-CQD respective zeta potential and charge readings were 41.3 and 48.2 mV. It has been postulated that articles with zeta potentials greater than +30 mV or more negative than −30 mV are typically regarded as stable in various investigations [39]. Consequently, the results show that Ber-CQD and Sr.Ber-CQD quantum dot are stable and inhibit sedimentation and agglomeration in liquids (Fig. 2B, D).

By examining the infrared spectrum of the FT-IR, it is possible to identify the chemical bonds, molecular interactions, and especially the type of functional groups unique to each material. In the study of the synthesis of CQD, in the FT-IR diagram of berberine (Fig. 3 a), wavenumber of 1506, 1569 cm−1 (CN), 1635 cm−1 (related to the double bond C = N+) and 2844 cm−1 (related to the methoxyl group R − O − CH 3) were simultaneously observed in the CQDs diagram, indicating that the main functional groups of berberine are also present (retained) in the synthesized CQDs structure. Also, in the diagram related to strontium nitrate, wavenumber in the range of 1330–1452 cm−1 (related to NO vibration) and intense absorption bands at 737 cm−1 and 815 cm−1 (related to the NO₃ bending state) were observed, and the presence of these wavenumber in the diagram related to CD indicates the presence of the strontium element in the synthesized quantum dot.

We applied XRD analysis to evaluate the crystallinity of the synthesized CQDs. The results (Fig. 3b) show that the CQDs exhibited two distinct peaks located around 24.9° and 40.1° that can be attributed to the (002) and (100) planes, respectively, of carbonaceous materials. These observations indicate the presence of a hexagonal graphite structure containing disordered carbon atoms and the formation of a turbostratic structure [38,40]. These results are in fair agreement with previous studies. Lu et al. [41] used citric acid in the presence of polyethylenimine to synthesize N-doped CQds using the hydrothermal method and reported a 2θ value of 20° for CDs.

A spectrophotometer was used to execute a complete scan from 200 to 800 nm in order to examine the optical properties of Sr.Ber-CQD. The results indicated a high optical absorption at wavelengths of 262, 375, and 420 nm. The most appropriate wavelength for excitation of the material was determined to be 375 nm (sharp, strong, and symmetrical black peak) based on the intensity and shape of the absorption peaks (Fig. 2d).

Fig. 3c compares the antioxidant capacity, or the ability to neutralize free radicals, of synthesized CQDs at varying doses (1000, 3000, 5000, and 100,000 μg/ml) to vitamin C (100 μg/ml) as the control group. The Fig. 3c illustrates how the antioxidant capacity rose to 55.48, 64.57, 71.23, and 72.42 %, respectively, when the concentration of Sr.Ber-CQD increased. According to some studies, berberine hydrochloride can counteract the consequences of oxidative stress by inhibiting malondialdehyde, boosting superoxide dismutase activity. An enzyme called superoxide dismutase catalyzes the conversion of the superoxide anion radical (O−2) into hydrogen peroxide and natural molecular oxygen (O2). Membrane lipid peroxidation produces malondialdehyde (MDA), a highly reactive bifunctional chemical. Red blood cell phospholipids and proteins are crosslinked by MDA, which impairs membrane-associated processes and ultimately lowers cell survival [42].

Berberine can suppress inflammation by blocking MAPK pathways in an AMPK-dependent manner and inhibiting the classical NF-kB signaling pathway. Studies have also shown that BBR administration activates the Nrf2 pathway, which is crucial for the antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities of BBR. The effect of BBR on Nrf2 relies on activating the AMPK, PI3K/Akt, and P38 pathways [42].

We applied XPS analysis to clarify the chemical composition of the synthesized CQDs. The results showed that the synthesized CQDs exhibited C1s, N1s, O1s, and Sr 3d signals (Fig. 4 ). The deconvoluted/high-resolution C1s spectrum displays three distinct peaks at 284.6 eV (CC), 285.6 eV (CSr, CN, and CO), and 287.9 eV (C=O). The deconvoluted/high-resolution N1s spectrum displays two distinct peaks at 400.5 eV (pyridinic Ns and NH2) and 401.8 eV (C(O)-N). The deconvoluted/high-resolution O1s spectrum displays two distinct peaks at 532.3 eV (C=O) and 533.4 eV (CO). The deconvoluted/high resolution Sr 3d spectrum displays two distinct peaks at 133.6 eV (Sr 3d5/2, SrO) and 135.1 eV (Sr 3d3/2, SrO).

Fig. 7 shows the EDAX spectrum of Sr.Ber-CQD nanofibers with different percentages, which indicate the presence of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and strontium elements. EDAX or EDS analysis uses the unique X-ray energy emitted from the sample to determine the type of element and its weight percentage as well as the degree of dispersion of elements in solid samples.

Fig. 8 c displays the FTIR spectra of PVA, SG, and nanofiber without CQD. Fig. 8d displays the FTIR spectra of CQDs, Nanofibers with 10 % CQD, and Nanofibers without CQD. Within the PVA graph, wavelengths 845, 1100, 1360, 1437, 1720, 2930, and 3340 cm−1 correspond to (CC) and (CO), (CH), (CH2), (C=O), (CH2), and (OH), respectively. In the scleroglucan graph, wavelengths 1040, 1645, and 2882 cm−1 correspond to (-C-O-C-), (OH group), and (CH). The presence of both materials and the preservation of the groups are confirmed by the observation of these peaks in the graph of nanofiber without CQD. their role in the nanofiber's synthesis. Also, the matching of the peaks of wavelengths 737, 815, 1102, 1380, 2946 cm−1 in the FTIR diagram of the nanofiber with 10 % CQD with the peaks of the FTIR spectrum of the nanofiber without CQD and the synthesized CQD showed that the synthesis of the nanofiber with 10 % CQD was successful.

For the nanofibers with varying percentages of Sr.Ber-CQD, stress-strain curves were created. The resultant plot (Fig. 9 a) shows that the nanofibers with percentages of 1 and 3 and 5 and 10 %, respectively, had strains of 9.9, 42.5, 68.1, and 9.1 MPa, and stress levels of 5.5, 5.1, 25.1, and 5.8. Tensile strengths with a minimum range of 6 to 18 MPa for nanofibers appropriate for skin wound dressing applications have been examined in a number of related investigations [43,44]. This demonstrates that our study's 5 % Sr.Ber-CQD nanofibers with the right amount of elasticity can withstand surface tension at the wound site.

The produced nanofibers' swelling test shows that they have an appropriate amount of water absorption when used as wound dressings. As stated in the Materials and Methods section, the gravimetric method was used to conduct this investigation. The 36-h swelling course of nanofibers with varying percentages of 0, 1, 3, 5, and 10 % Ber.Sr-CQD in PBS at room temperature is depicted in Fig. 9d. Because PVA and SG are hydrophilic, water was able to permeate into their fibers and cause swelling that grew over the course of 24 h. The findings demonstrated that the nanofibers with 3 and 5 % Sr.Ber-CQD had the greatest swelling capability, suggesting that this dressing is appropriate for absorbing wound exudate.

The produced nanofibers were kept at room temperature for 14 days in phosphate buffer with a pH of 7.4 in order to examine their biodegradability. For nanofibers with percentages of 5 and 1 %, respectively, the lowest and greatest biodegradability values over a 14-day period were 41.40 ± 1.94 and 88.48 ± 2.93, all of which were within the permissible range (Fig. 9c). The findings demonstrated that the biodegradability value did not approach equilibrium and instead grew steadily over the course of 14 days. One of the key considerations in the design of novel dressings is the biodegradability rate of the nanofibers, as evidenced by the release of Sr.Ber-CQD in the electrospun nanofibers following their biodegradation. Various factors such as hydrophilicity, crosslinking degree, polymer density, temperature, pH, etc. can affect this index.

The ability of the synthesized nanofibers to absorb wound exudates was assessed using the liquid displacement method, which also looked at the presence of porosity in the produced nanofibers in liquid absorption. As illustrated in Fig. 9b, every nanofiber exhibited porosity >67 %, with 0 % and 5 % nanofibers exhibiting the maximum porosity at 70.78 ± 0.55 % and 69.57 ± 1.64 %, respectively. According to the findings, the nanofibers appear to be a good fit for absorbing wound exudate.

In order to avoid exudate buildup or dehydration of the wound surface, which both raise the risk of infection and scarring at the wound site, an appropriate dressing for exuding wounds should be able to maintain a moisture balance between the surrounding environment and the wound surface. Consequently, one of the most important aspects of enhancing the quality of wound healing is managing wound moisture. The WVTR values are determined by the hydrophilicity of the biomaterial and the structural characteristics of the nanofiber, including its thickness and porosity [45].

A healthy wound has a WVTR rate of 204 g/m2/24 h. Exudate builds up at the interface between the wound and the wound covering at values below this threshold, which can create an ideal habitat for pathogen proliferation and infection progression. In order to avoid exudate buildup or wound dryness, appropriate wound coverings should have a greater WVTR rate than normal skin, with an average rate of 2500 g/m2/24 h advised [46].

The water vapor permeability of nanofibers with varying percentages of Ber.Sr of 0, 1, 3, 5, and 10 was examined in this work using the water vapor permeability rate (WVTR). According to the data, the nanofibers with 0 % Ber.Sr (g/m2/24 h 396.25 ± 2049.24) and 10 % Ber.Sr (g/m2/24 h 757.42 ± 3092.05) had the lowest and greatest WVTR values after 24 h, respectively (Fig. 10 ).While there was no discernible difference across the nanofiber groups with varying Ber.Sr percentages, all of the nanofiber groups' WVTRs differed significantly from the control group (OPC) (P < 0.0001: ****).

The hemolysis rate of blood in contact with the nanofiber dressing at the wound site was examined using a blood compatibility test. The hemolysis rate must be <2 % to verify that the material is not hemolytic. According to Bailey et al.'s study, rough surfaces are less likely to cause thrombosis [47]. Additionally, according to Vincent (2012), blood compatibility is impacted by the fibers' short diameter [39].

Fig. 11a and b display the hemolysis rate data generated by nanofibers with 0, 1, 3, 5, and 10 % Sr.Ber-CQD. The hemolysis rates for each of the nanofibers in this investigation were <2 % (0.86, 1.60, 1.43, 1.48, and 1.69 %, respectively). As can be seen from the results, the hemolysis rate increased as the percentage of Sr.Ber-CQD in the nanofibers increased. However, no significant difference was observed between the groups; however, all different percentages of nanofibers demonstrated a significant difference when compared to the positive control group (P < 0.0001: ****). The outcomes showed that the produced nanofibers had good wound healing and could be used as dressings. According to some studies, red blood cell phospholipids and proteins are cross-linked by malondialdehyde (MDA), which disrupts membrane-related processes and ultimately lowers cell survival and causes red blood cell lysis. However, by inhibiting malondialdehyde, berberine hydrochloride can shield red blood cells from hemolysis caused by MDA [42].

One of the most important things to consider in designing new dressings is the ability of the dressing to prevent the passage of external pathogens such as bacteria, as well as the antibacterial properties of their ingredients to prevent the penetration and growth of microorganisms. In the present study, all designed nanofibers with 0, 1, 3, 5, and 10 % Sr.Ber-CQD percentages and negative control groups (with a tight plastic cap) and positive control (without a cap) were evaluated for 7 days. On days 3 and 7, the absorption of TSB broth culture medium into the vials and the number of possible colonies in blood agar culture medium were examined. The measured absorption rates on days 3 and 7 are shown in Fig. 12 , which indicates that the absorption of the negative control sample and the nanofiber-capped groups is significantly lower than the absorption of the positive control group on days 3 and 7 (P < 0.0001: ****).

As can be seen in the graph, the lowest absorption rates on days 3 and 7 are for the negative control group and the 5 % and 10 % nanofibers. The TSB culture medium inside the vials was cultured on blood agar and EMB culture media on days 3 and 7 (Fig. 13 ). On days 3 and 7, the number of colonies grown on blood agar and EMB agar media from all vials was examined.

As can be seen in Fig. 14a and b, on days 3 and 7, only the sample from the positive control vial caused the growth of microorganisms on blood agar and EMB media. The antibacterial properties of nanofibers containing berberine quantum dots were investigated by Agar Diffusion Assay. As shown in Fig. 14 and Table 1 , the bacterial growth inhibition zone increases with increasing Sr.Ber-CQD percentage in nanofibers, indicating the antibacterial properties of berberine in nanofibers. The results of the microbial penetration analysis and agar diffusion assay tests showed that due to the electrospun structure of the fibers (thickness, porosity, etc.) and the presence of berberine with high antibacterial properties in the nanofiber dressing, this dressing effectively prevents the passage and growth of pathogens and can be used to protect wounds against infection. Several studies have reported that the mechanism of antibacterial activity of berberine is related to its ability to adsorb positive nitrogen cations on the surface of negatively charged bacterial cells. It can compete with the coenzyme pyridoxal phosphate and interfere with the membrane surface [30]. Several in vitro studies have shown that metal ions such as strontium, alone or in combination with other metal ions, have a reasonable antimicrobial effect. Strontium interacts with the bacterial cell wall and changes the structure of the cell membrane and/or damages DNA, leading to cell death or inhibition of bacterial proliferation. The results of various studies have shown that strontium hydroxide has a bacteriostatic and bactericidal effect at certain concentrations [48].

The ability to neutralize free radicals, or the antioxidant capacity, of nanofibers synthesized with 0, 1, 3, 5, and 10 % Sr.Ber-CQD and its comparison with the negative control is shown in Fig. 15 . Comparing the results of nanofibers with different percentages of Sr.Ber-CQD with the negative control group (nanofiber without Sr.Ber-CQD) showed a P value of <0.0001 (p < 0.0001) ****.Nanofibers with 5 and 10 % Sr.Ber-CQD showed the highest antioxidant properties compared to the negative control group with 53.33 and 60.72 %, respectively (****p < 0.0001).

The imbalance between oxidants and antioxidants when oxidative stress occurs can disrupt the process of accelerating and healing wounds by having a destructive effect on DNA, RNA, and various proteins. One of the main antioxidants in redox regulation in mammalian cells, which is encoded by the TXNRD1 gene, is called thioredoxin reductase 1, or TrxR1 for short. Various studies have shown that TrxR1, low signaling It inhibits JNK, which inhibits oxidative stress. It also increases cell proliferation, down-regulates matrix metalloproteinase (MMP9), and up-regulates transforming growth factor-β1 (TGF-β1), leading to accelerated wound healing. MMP9 destroys type IV collagen, which is the main material of the basement membrane. Also, the TGF-β1 molecule, as a cytokine with diverse actions including modulation and suppression of inflammation, plays an important role in the process of skin inflammation, so the anti-inflammatory role of this molecule and its receptors has been seen in skin inflammation and common diseases. The present study showed that Sr.Ber-CQD with appropriate antioxidant properties can prevent the adverse effects of oxidative stress and impaired wound healing, so the control of free radicals is essential in wound healing [49].Overall, the results of investigating the ability of Sr.Ber-CQD in nanofibers to neutralize free radicals and suppress and inhibit oxidative stress to a significant extent allow us to conclude that these materials can play a role in accelerating and healing wounds.

In vitro rate and speed of wound closure were examined using the Cell Migration Assay, also known as the Scratch Test. In this test, nanofiber extract was utilized in place of nanofibers, as they make it impossible to observe the rate at which cell scratches close near the bottom of the plates. In contrast to the control group, which contained DMEM medium and received no treatment (p-value <0.0001, p-value <0.001, respectively), the group with 5 % Sr.Ber-CQD demonstrated the highest percentage of closure at 12 and 24 h after scratch creation, with 39.54 % and 67.77 %, respectively, as illustrated in Fig. 16a and b. In the presence of culture medium containing nanofiber extract and DMEM medium as a control (without Sr. Ber-CQD), Fig. 16a illustrates cell migration to the wound area and the closure of the scratch formed within 12 and 24 h. As can be seen from the pictures, the nanofiber extract containing 5 % Sr.Ber-CQD had the biggest impact of all the groups, repairing 67.77 % of the scratches made in comparison to the control group. The results of this investigation align with those of related investigations. In 2020, for instance, Shangjun Fu and his associates demonstrated that Berb pretreatment can enhance cell migration and that the effect of encouraging scratch closure grows as scratch time increases [50]. Additionally, Karthikeyan Kandaswamy and associates demonstrated that applying berberine to the scratch produced during the scratch test can accelerate the healing process. When combined, these findings suggest that berberine use can speed up the healing process of wounds [51].

The MTT assay was used to examine the produced nanofibers' biocompatibility. According to some research, substances are deemed non-toxic if their cell viability is >80 % [33]. Significant variations were seen in the vitality of fibroblast cells by nanofibers made with varying percentages of 0, 1, 3, 5, and 10 of Sr.Ber-CQD at 24, 48, and 72 h following cell implantation. Additionally, according to Tukey's Post Hoc test, there was a significant difference in viability between the control group and the 3 %, 5 %, and 10 % groups 24 h after cell implantation (p-value <0.01, p-value <0.01, and p-value <0.05, respectively). Survival was significantly different between the control group and the 3 % (p-value <0.001), 5 %, and 10 % (p-value <0.0001). groups 48 h after cell implantation, and between the control group and the 1 % (p-value <0.05), 3 %, 5 %, and 10 % (p-value <0.0001) groups 72 h after cell implantation. The percentage of fibroblast cells that survive at 24, 48, and 72 h following cell implantation is displayed in (Fig. 16c): The outcomes are in line with earlier research; for instance, Zhengbo Hu et al.'s study demonstrated that berberine's biocompatibility was >70 %, demonstrating the non-toxicity of the intended wound dressing [52].

Furthermore, several investigations have demonstrated that berberine is cytotoxic to cancer cell lines, with dose- and time-dependent effects. Numerous mechanisms of berberine's anticancer activity have been demonstrated by studies, including the use of high doses of the herb, which can be toxic to both human and animal cells due to potential stimulatory mechanisms that result in cell cycle arrest, DNA damage, changes in the potential of the mitochondrial membrane, and cell apoptosis [53–55].Cell viability was unaffected by the modest amounts of Sr.Ber-CQD used in the produced nanofibers in the current investigation.

A full-thickness wound animal model in Wistar rats was employed to mimic biological conditions to assess the safety and effectiveness of the manufactured nanofibrous dressing. In addition to inflammatory responses and molecular and cellular alterations in the wound environment, this model accurately depicts the macromolecular processes of wound closure. Fig. 18a and b present the findings. The pictures show that the wound site is free of infection and irritation.

A commercial wound dressing model (Coloplast Comfeel brand) and a control group (which received no therapy and just sterile gauze) were utilized for 14 days in order to examine and contrast the effectiveness of the synthesized nanofibrous wound dressing. Both qualitative and quantitative methods were used to assess the findings. On day 14, as illustrated in Fig. 17a, b, and c, the wound closure rate in the commercial group and the nanofiber group with 5 % Sr.CQD-Ber was 91.56 % and 89.22 % higher, respectively, than the control group (without treatment and dressing) and the nanofiber group without Sr.Ber-CQD (p-value <0.0001).

The nanofibers wound dressing containing 5 %Ber.Sr-CQD exhibits a more efficient healing compare to NF/0%Ber.Sr-CQD and control group. On day 3, the nanofiber with 5 %Ber.Sr-CQD showed 14.22 % wound closure that is closely to commercial group with 15.66 %, while the NF/0%Ber.Sr-CQD achieved only 5.56 %. This difference that is noticeable in the early wound stages confirms the important role of Ber in improve and accelerating of wound repair. After 14 days, nanofiber with 5 % Ber.Sr-CQD reached 89.22 % wound closure, just merely 2.34 % lower than commercial group with 91.56 % and more significant higher than 68.16 % of control group. On the other hand, despite of weaker initial performance in wound healing, by day 14 NF/0%Ber.Sr-CQD surpassed the control group with 73.49 % closure that this result highlighting the positive influence of nanofiber mechanical structure in wound repair. Comparing these groups together underscores the nanofiber Mechanical and structural properties's inherent ability to facilitate wound healing even without Ber. These observations show that the 5 % Ber.Sr-CQD nanofiber structure synergistically leverages Ber properties (such as antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory) by providing a microenvironment that is favorable for cellular regeneration.

The conclusions point out that synergistic interactions between bioactive compounds and nanofibers design play a significant role in designing neowound dressings, and emphasize that optimizing drug compounds and concentrations in such systems can enhance therapeutic efficacy. Overall, the animal model's findings suggest that an electrospun nanofiber dressing containing 5 % Sr.Ber-CQD may be a good choice for enhancing and quickening the healing process of wounds. Our findings are supported by similar research that used berberine as a natural supplement in the dressing's design. For example, Panda et al. found that combining berberine with chitosan polymer improved wound healing in diabetic mice by lowering inflammatory cells and boosting collagen expression [56]. Furthermore, Zhang et al. found that berberine lowers inflammatory factors by influencing Sirt1 (silent information regulator 1) expression, which raises angiogenic factor expression and promotes wound healing [57].

We applied the Hematoxylin and eosin staining (H&E) staining to evaluate the histological changes on days 7th and 14th. Additionally, MT staining was used to mark the distribution and synthesis of collagen fibers throughout the healing process. The results (Figs. 18 and 19
) show that the control group did not develop an epidermal layer and had a clear invasion of inflammatory cells. However, the lesion was treated with the nanofibrous wound dressing containing 5 % CQDs and showed the most extraordinary likeness to normal skin (full epidermal layer development, decreased inflammatory cell infiltration, and the presence of sebaceous glands and hair follicles). The nanofibrous wound dressing containing 5 % CQDs showed the maximum collagen production, maturity, and orientation, according to the MT staining, whereas the control group showed the lowest collagen deposition and maturation. The loading of CQDs considerably increased the healing efficacy, even if the pure nanofibers improved the healing process. Angiogenesis and neovascularization, which supply oxygen and metabolites to the organs and tissues, are essential for the survival of cells such as fibroblasts and keratinocytes, neoformed granulation, and tissue regeneration.

In conclusion, the goal of this study was to create a novel nanofibrous wound dressing with strontium-doped berberine quantum dot, and it was effectively completed. The optimal synthesis technique for Sr.Ber-CQDs was chosen based on the results of the initial synthesis and testing of the particles using characterization tests (such as FT-IR, TEM, XRD, DLS, zeta potential, etc.). Then, different percentages of electrospun nanofibers were created and tested using various methods (SEM, EDX, FT-IR, XRD, tensile strength, water uptake, WVTR, weight loss, porosity, blood compatibility, microbial penetration, and DPPH). It was discovered that the synthesized nanofibers, particularly those containing 5 % Sr.Ber-CQDs, could boost wound closure, collagen synthesis, re-epithelialization, and neovascularization, thereby enhancing the rate and quality of wound healing in both in vitro and animal models.

Further research on the anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial effects of berberine as well as the angiogenic effects of strontium was then carried out using DPPH, agar diffusion assay, histopathology tests, and other methods. Based on the findings, these nanofibers might be a promising new therapeutic approach for treating full-thickness wound healing. The findings of this study have practical implications for nanofibrous wound dressing, offering a potential alternative for accelerating wound healing. The findings of this study confirm and extend previous research on nanofibrous wound dressing combined with antioxidant nanoparticles for the healing and management of wounds. Using different characterization techniques in this study provides a strong basis for the findings. The study's novel approach to combining antioxidant and antigenic carbon-based nanostructure with nanofibrous wound dressing provides a fresh perspective on nanocomposite wound healing materials. Moreover, the rigorous statistical analysis employed in this study enhances the validity and reliability of the results, reducing the possibility of bias. Despite the indicated strengths, the present study has some limitations that should be addressed in future studies. The lack of mechanistic studies that can be addressed using molecular analysis (qPCR, immunohistochemical tests, etc.), conducting proper preclinical studies and clinical trials, lack of evaluating long-term outcomes, such as scar quality and tissue remodeling, insufficient in vivo toxicity and biodegradation assessments, and insufficient dose optimization for in vivo applications. However, the findings of this study represent a significant advancement in the creation of novel and effective coatings to enhance the healing of acute wounds, including full-thickness wounds.

Masoud Karimi: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Mehdi Azizi: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Masoud Hamidi: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Razieh Dalirfardouei: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation. Hadi Samadian: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization. Akram Jalali: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization.
Ethical consideration related to the study was approved by the Research Ethics Committee of Hamadan University of Medical Sciences (IR.UMSHA.AEC.1402.013).
